coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of mesoporous ion-imprinted fluorescent sensors for rapid and reliable Cu2+ detection in aqueous environments
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Jiaqi
Zhao, Lingxi
Zhang, Tianyi
Yang, Zhu
Liu, Xiangnan
Hu, Bo
Zhao, Lun
Che, Guangbo
coredata.dc:description: A novel mesoporous ion-imprinted fluorescent sensor (SiO2@CDs@MIIPs) was developed for the detection of Cu2+ in aqueous environments. This sensor was constructed by modifying mesoporous silica nanospheres with blue-emitting carbon dots (CDs), which serve as both a fluorescence source and a functional platform. The mesoporous structure ensures efficient ion recognition and facilitates rapid mass transfer, while the integration of CDs enhances fluorescence stability. The sensor demonstrated a detection limit of 5.74 μM, a linear range of 20–100 μM, and a rapid response time of 3 min. Although the selectivity and sensitivity are moderate, the unique combination of mesoporous silica and CDs provides a stable and practical solution for Cu2+ detection. Validation using tap water and river water samples confirmed its reliability, with results aligning closely with those obtained by ICP-MS. This work highlights the potential of functionalized silica-based nanomaterials for environmental monitoring and offers a promising direction for the design of ion-imprinted fluorescent sensors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Mesoporous silica
Carbon dots
Ion imprinted polymers
Copper ion detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Study on the detection performance of SiO2@CDs@MIIPs for Cu2+
Study on the mechanism of fluorescence quenching
Comparison of response time and detection limit

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of CDs (a), SiO2@CDs (b), SiO2@CDs@IIPs (c) and SiO2@CDs@MIIPs (d), SEM images of SiO2 (b) and SiO2@CDs@IIPs (c) and dynamic light scattering size distribution of SiO2@CDs (g), SiO2@CDs@IIPs (h) and SiO2@CDs@MIIPs (i).
N2 adsorption/desorption curves of SiO2@CDs@MIIPs (a), pore size distribution maps of SiO2@CDs@MIIPs (b) and FT-IR spectra of SiO2, SiO2@CDs, SiO2@CDs@MIIPs and SiO2@CDs@MNIPs (c).

Fluorescence contour map of CDs (a), combination diagram of the absorption spectrum, fluorescence excitation spectrum, and fluorescence emission spectrum of CDs (b), effect of the concentration of SiO2@CDs@MIIPs on fluorescence intensity and detection (c), Effect of pH on fluorescence intensity of SiO2@CDs@MIIPs (d), Fluorescent intensities of SiO2@CDs@MIIPs in 60 min (e) and Fluorescent intensity of SiO2@CDs@MIIPs in the presence of 50 μM Cu2+ at different incubation time (f).

Fluorescence spectra of SiO2@CDs@MIIPs (a), SiO2@CDs@MNIPs (c) and SiO2@CDs@IIPs (e) in the concentration range of Cu2+ from 0 to 100 μM (the inset shows the actual fluorescence color change of the assay solution at the corresponding concentration),Linear relationship of SiO2@CDs@MIIPs (b), SiO2@CDs@MNIPs (d) and SiO2@CDs@IIPs (f) upon the same concentration range of Cu2+.

(a)Degree of fluorescence quenching of SiO2@CDs@MIIPs in ionic solutions containing 50 μM Na+, Ca2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Cr2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Al3+, Ni2+, SO4 2−, CO3 2−, HCO3 −, Cl− and NO3 −. (b)Fluorescence lifetimes of CDs (red square) and CDs with Cu2+ (blue square), the corresponding first-order fitting curves of CDs (pink line) and CDs with Cu2+ (green line).
Comparison of SiO2@CDs@MIIPs with other fluorescence sensors.
Comparisons of Cu2+ determination results in drinking actual samples by fluorescence analysis method and ICP-MS method.

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: This article is part of a special issue entitled: Environment nanomaterials published in Results in Surfaces and Interfaces.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of mesoporous ion-imprinted fluorescent sensors for rapid and reliable Cu2+ detection in aqueous environments

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Clean Energy in Western Jilin Province
School of Environment and Safety Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel mesoporous ion-imprinted fluorescent sensor (SiO2@CDs@MIIPs) was developed for the detection of Cu2+ in aqueous environments. This sensor was constructed by modifying mesoporous silica nanospheres with blue-emitting carbon dots (CDs), which serve as both a fluorescence source and a functional platform. The mesoporous structure ensures efficient ion recognition and facilitates rapid mass transfer, while the integration of CDs enhances fluorescence stability. The sensor demonstrated a detection limit of 5.74 μM, a linear range of 20–100 μM, and a rapid response time of 3 min. Although the selectivity and sensitivity are moderate, the unique combination of mesoporous silica and CDs provides a stable and practical solution for Cu2+ detection. Validation using tap water and river water samples confirmed its reliability, with results aligning closely with those obtained by ICP-MS. This work highlights the potential of functionalized silica-based nanomaterials for environmental monitoring and offers a promising direction for the design of ion-imprinted fluorescent sensors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Copper is an essential trace element for living organisms, playing a vital role in various biological processes, including oxidative metabolism, photosynthesis, carbohydrate and protein metabolism, cell wall formation, and symbiotic nitrogen fixation [1]. Although moderate amounts of heavy elements help to meet everyday human physiological needs, excessive copper ion uptake produces toxic disruption of intracellular copper homeostasis. Thereby affecting the growth and development of organisms. Eventually, it is enriched in the human body through the biological chain, threatening human health [2–4]. With the mining of copper and the discharge of copper-containing industrial wastewater, the heavy metal element copper has been accumulating in the water environment in the form of ions. Copper pollution is subtly affecting our daily lives [5]. Therefore, the frequent detection of Cu2+ in the water body of each link is of great application value.

Copper ion detection methods are classified into laboratory and on-site detection methods according to the application scenarios. Among the widely used laboratory methods include inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [6], atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) [7], ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometry (UV-VIS) [8] and electrochemical methods [9]. While these methods offer high sensitivity and precision, they often require expensive instruments, specialized handling, and complex pre-treatment processes. ICP-MS, for example, provides excellent accuracy but is costly and unsuitable for field use. AAS, while less expensive, has lower sensitivity and also requires laboratory conditions. UV-VIS is more affordable and simpler to operate but is less accurate in the presence of interfering substances. Electrochemical methods, although suitable for on-site detection, can be affected by environmental factors and may require careful calibration [10–12]. Therefore, developing on-site detection methods for Cu2+ and other heavy metal ions, with results close to those of laboratory methods, which can reduce detection costs and increase the effective sampling rate, is an urgent need for the industry today.

Ion Imprinting Technology (IIT) is a novel separation identification technique based on bionic learning of enzyme-substrate and antigen-antibody interactions in nature [13]. Ion Imprinted polymers (IIPs), prepared based on IIT have large adsorption capacity, selectivity and environmental stability. Widely used in separation and purification, contaminant identification, etc. The combination of IIT and fluorescence analysis for the preparation of Fluorescent IIPs for the in situ detection of metal ions not only retains the advantages of the fluorescence method but also effectively reduces the interference of the complex sample matrix and the non-laboratory environment on the detection results. IIPs are a coupled product of IIT and fluorescence analysis technology. It is based on the selective recognition of specific recognition sites in ion-imprinted fluorescence sensors and visualization of the fluorescence sensing process. The process mimics the detection aspect of pre-processing in laboratory testing methods. IIPs provide the basis for visual in situ detection of Cu2+ in environmental waters without sophisticated instrumentation.

In order to overcome the interference of various metal cations present in the natural environment on the fluorescence analysis method, IIT has been used to construct a donor-acceptor blotting recognition system to form ion blotting fluorescence sensors with specific recognition ability to realize the selective detection of Cu2+. Carbon dots (CDs), a new type of carbon material with good fluorescent properties, can be easily hydrogen-bonded with other substances to construct fluorescent functional materials because their shells are occupied by hydrogen atoms or functional groups such as –NH2, –OH, –COOH. CDs are highly susceptible to hydrogen bonding with other substances to build fluorescent functional materials [14–18]. Fluorescent nanoparticles constructed based on silanes and CDs have been widely used to detect heavy metal ions in the environment. It has also been used as a substrate material for preparing IIPs by surface lotting [19–22]. IIPs have a slow relative response rate and low sensitivity since the fluorescence-functionalized substrate material that serves as the recognition signal is encapsulated in the imprinted layer. This is detrimental to the rapid response to Cu2+, limiting its use in real-world environment. However, mesoporous silica material prepared based on surface imprinting technology has a uniform mesoporous structure, which can bind template ions more effectively and shorten the distance from the imprinted layer to the fluorescence recognition signal. Thus shorten the response time of fluorescence sensing [23–28].

This study synthesized a novel mesoporous ion-imprinted fluorescent sensor (SiO2@CDs@MIIPs) based on SiO2 nano microspheres SiO2@CDs modified with blue fluorescence-emitting CDs on the surface. It is used to detect Cu2+ in actual samples. CDs with abundant –COOH and –NH3 groups on the surface were modified on the surface of SiO2 nano microspheres by covalent bonding. SiO2@CDs@MIIPs were successfully synthesized on the surface of SiO2@CDs by surface blotting method using 3-Aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) as the functional monomer, ethyl silicate (TEOS) as the crosslinking agent, Cu2+ as the template ion. At the same time, cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) is the porogenic agent. SiO2@CDs@MIIPs without mesoporous structure were also prepared. The process examined the effect of mesopore structure on the pore size, adsorption and specific surface area of the imprinted sensor. The impact of the modulation of the thickness of the blotting layer on the selectivity and immunity of fluorescent blotting sensors is demonstrated by comparing the fluorescence detection performance of non-mesoporous blotting for Cu2+. Finally, it was used to rapidly determine Cu2+ in tap and river water. The fluorescence results were validated using ICP-MS. (The synthesis routes of SiO2@CDs@MIIPs are shown in Fig. 1 ).

Citric acid anhydrous (C6H8O7), carbamide (CH4N2O), tetraethyl orthosilicate (C8H12O8Si, TEOS), aqueous ammonia (H5NO), cupric chloride (CuCl2), 3-Aminopropylmethyldimethoxysilane (C9H23NO3Si, APTES), hexadecyl trimethyl ammonium Bromide (C19H42BrN, CTAB), 2-Morpholinoethanesulfonic Acid (C6H13NO4S, MES), 1-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethyl carbodiimide hydrochloride (C8H18ClN3, EDC) and N-Hydroxy succinimide (C4H5NO3) were acquired from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., LTD. In comparison, ethyl alcohol (C2H6O), sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid (C10H16N2O8, EDTA) were provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. LTD. The deionized water involved was acquired within an internal purification system.

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded using a Nicolet iS50 spectrometer (Thermo Scientific). The morphology and particle size of the samples were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JEOL JSM-7800F) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2010HR). Fluorescence measurements were carried out using an F-98 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shanghai Lengguang Technology Co., Ltd.).

Amino acid-modified CDs were synthesized with appropriate modifications from the known literature. Dissolve 0.5 g of anhydrous citric acid and 0.5 g of carbamide in 12.5 mL of deionized water by ultrasonication with magnetic stirring for 30 min. Transfer the mixed solution to a stainless steel autoclave with a 30 mL PTFE liner, heating at 160 °C for 5.0 h by hydrothermal method. After completion of the reaction, the reaction was cooled naturally to room temperature. The reaction solution containing CDs was dialyzed using a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cut-off of 1000. Deionized water was changed every 8.0 h for three days under magnetic stirring conditions. The final post-dialysis solution is transferred to a refrigerator at −80 °C for rapid freezing, lyophilized and stored away from light.

SiO2 nano microspheres are used as substrate material for CDs and surface blotting using the Stöber method. Accurately measure 1.5 mL of TEOS, adding dropwise to a 100 mL three-necked flask containing 50 mL of anhydrous ethanol. After magnetic stirring for 3.0 min, 2.0 mL of ammonia was added dropwise. After 24 h of sealed reaction at room temperature, the reaction products were collected by centrifugation. The reaction products were washed thrice with deionized water and anhydrous ethanol. Finally, the obtained products will be dried under a vacuum.1.0 mg of CDs and 20 mg of SiO2 nanorods were dispersed in 20 mL of MES (pH = 6.1) buffer solution. Then, 1.0 mL of EDC/NHS (mg/mL) mixed solution was added. The reaction products were collected by centrifugation after 2.0 h of magnetic stirring at room temperature. Then, the reaction products were collected by centrifugation. Unreacted material was removed by washing three times with deionized water and anhydrous ethanol.

The pre-polymerization system was constructed by adding 0.24 mL of APTES and 0.07 g of CuCl2 to a beaker containing 15 mL of deionized water and 35 mL of anhydrous ethanol. After magnetic stirring for 20 min at room temperature, 0.19 g CTAB was added. After that, TEOS 0.6 mL was added drop by drop. Measure 18.18 mg of SiO2@CDs prepared in step 2.4, add it into the mixed solution as substrate, and ultrasonically disperse it for 10 min. Subsequently, 2.0 mL of NaOH solution (1 mol/L) was added. The sample was magnetically stirred for 24 h at room temperature. The reaction products were collected by centrifugation at 10000 rpm. The template ions Cu2+ and CTAB were then removed by refluxing for 8 h using the Soxhlet extraction method using 0.2 mol/L EDTA as solvent. Finally, the products were washed with deionized water to neutralize them. Then, it was dried under a vacuum to obtain SiO2@CDs@MIIPs.

Studying the fluorescence properties of SiO2@CDs@MIIPs is crucial for their successful application in field detection. SiO2@CDs@MIIPs were ultrasonically dispersed, configuring as a 0.5 g/L aqueous solution stocked in a cuvette, followed by the configuration of a specific range of concentration gradient of 10 mL of aqueous Cu2+ solution as the solution to be measured, 200 μL of the stock solution were added to the solution to be measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer. The linear equations of SiO2@CDs@MIIPs and Cu2+ concentration were plotted according to the fluorescence intensity of different concentrations of Cu2+.

The ICP-MS detection method for Cu2+ was based on the “Standard Test Methods for Drinking Water Part 6: Metals and Metalloids Indicators (GB/T 5750.6–2023)" issued by the National Health and Sanitation Commission with appropriate modifications. The operating conditions of the ICP-MS were set as follows: plasma mode HM1,F matching voltage set to 1.80 Voltage, peristaltic pump speed 0.10 r/s,cooling gas flow rate 15.0 L/min,nebulizer gas flow rate 0.56 L/min,auxiliary gas flow rate 0.90 L/min,collision cell He flow rate 5.0 mL/min and sampling depth 10.0 mm. The actual samples were taken from the Yudai River of Jiangsu University. The macromolecular impurities in the samples were removed through the filter membrane before the test. The electroplating wastewater was taken from an electroplating limited company in Zhenjiang City. The samples were diluted 1000 times before the test,and the macromolecular impurities were removed through the filter membrane.

Fig. 2 shows the SEM, TEM and particle size analysis of the products that prepare SiO2@CDs@MIIPs and SiO2@CDs@IIPs. Fig. 2a shows a TEM photograph of CDs. The size of the prepared material is minimal, about 2–5 nm in diameter, which is consistent with the characteristics of CDs. Fig. 2b shows the SEM photo of SiO2. The prepared SiO2 has a spherical morphology, smooth surface, uniform size and good monodispersity. Fig. 2d shows the TEM photo of SiO2@CDs. It can be seen that the CDs grow uniformly on the surface of SiO2. There are no CDs in the blank area around SiO2@CDs, which indicates that the CDs are chemically covalently modified on the surface of SiO2 rather than a simple physical doping form. Combined with the particle size analysis of SiO2@CDs in Fig. 2g, it can be seen that the average particle size of SiO2@CDs is 95 nm, with uniform size and good monodispersity, which indicates that the modification of CDs did not lead to the formation of agglomerates of SiO2@CDs. Fig. 2c and e shows the SEM and TEM photographs of SiO2@CDs@IIPs as comparisons, respectively. From Fig. 2c, it can be observed that the surface of SiO2@CDs@IIPs is much rougher, which suggests that a polymer layer is formed on the surface of the SiO2@CDs substrate after the polymerization reaction. The formation of a thin outer polymer layer is also evidenced by the apparent layered structure in the TEM images, with the thickness of the polymer layer being about 15 nm. Fig. 2f shows the TEM image of mesoporous imprinted polymer SiO2@CDs@MIIPs, from which the pore structure produced by the porogenic agent CTAB can be observed. The imprinted layer of SiO2@CDs@MIIPs is thicker than the non-mesoporous imprinted polymers of about 80 nm. This will effectively protect the CDs on the SiO2 surface, increase the spatial distance between the interfering ions and small molecules and the CDs, impede the fluorescence quenching due to the Stokes shift, and improve the selectivity to Cu2+. Also, an increase in the thickness of the imprinted layer will increase the number of specific recognition sites, which combined with the mesoporous structure, improves the adsorption of Cu2+ and enhances the assay's sensitivity.

To further investigate the effect of mesoporous structure on the imprinted layer, BET tests were performed on SiO2@CDs@MIIP. The N2 adsorption-desorption curves and pore size distributions are shown in Fig. 3 From the typical hysteresis loop in the N2 adsorption-desorption curve after P/P0 is more significant than 0.9, it can be seen that the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm of SiO2@CDs@MIIPs conforms to the IV-type curve defined by the International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC), which is a typical mesoporous structure N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm. The surface area of SiO2@CDs@MIIPs can be calculated as 920.187 m2/g by the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method. Using the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) method, the average pore diameter of SiO2@CDs@MIIPs is 2.74 nm and the pore volume is 2.198 cm3/g.

The infrared spectra of SiO2, SiO2@CDs, SiO2@CDs@MIIPs and SiO2@CDs@MNIPs are shown in Fig. 3, were used to study the internal structure of SiO2@CDs@MIIPs. As shown in the figure, all the samples contain 1083 cm−1, 788 cm−1 and 437 cm−1. The three characteristic peaks are attributed to the deformation vibrational absorption peaks and telescopic vibrational peaks of Si–O–Si, which indicate that all the samples have a silica-oxygen skeleton structure. The firm absorption peaks at 1635 and 3450 cm−1 for the red line compared to the black line can be attributed to the telescopic vibrational peaks of –NH3, which is due to the characteristic peak changes on the SiO2 surface produced by the modification of the surface amino-rich CDs. These results show that since both the imprinted layer and the substrate material are silicon-based and the mesoporous structure does not change the chemical composition of the imprinted layer, the infrared spectra of the imprinted polymer and the substrate material do not change significantly.

Before using SiO2@CDs@MIIPs as a fluorescence detector for detecting Cu2+, the fluorescence properties and optimal detection conditions of SiO2@CDs@ MIIPs should be explored. The optimal fluorescence excitation wavelength of SiO2@CDs@MIIPs was first determined by studying the fluorescence properties of CDs. Fig. 4 shows the combined fluorescence contour map (a), UV absorption spectrum, fluorescence excitation spectrum and fluorescence emission spectrum (b) of CDs. From the spectral data in the figure, it can be seen that the UV absorption peak of CDs is 237 nm. The optimal excitation wavelength for the fluorescence of CDs is 297 nm. The emission wavelength was 455 nm.

The concentration of the fluorescent sensor in the detection solution affects the fluorescence intensity and sensitivity of the detection system. To determine the optimal working concentration of SiO2@CDs@MIIPs in the detection solution and to improve the detection of Cu2+, an aqueous solution of SiO2@CDs@MIIPs with a concentration gradient of 10–100 mg/L was configured in this experiment. A fluorescence spectrophotometer was used to record the original fluorescence intensity F
0 of the test solution and the fluorescence intensity F by adding 50 μM Cu2+, respectively. The fluorescence intensity of SiO2@CDs@MIIPs and their corresponding (F
0
–F)/F
0 at different concentrations were also investigated to determine the optimal working concentration of SiO2@CDs@MIIPs in the detection solution. As shown in Fig. 4c, the corresponding fluorescence intensity of SiO2@CDs@MIIPs increased with the initial concentration of SiO2@CDs@MIIPs. At 100 mg/L SiO2@CDs@MIIPs concentration, the fluorescence intensity of the assay solution still did not decrease, which indicates that the aggregation-induced quenching effect of SiO2@CDs@MIIPs does not occur in this concentration range. Larger values of (F
0
–F)/F
0 indicate higher fluorescence quenching of SiO2@CDs@MIIPs due to Cu2+. The larger the value of (F
0
–F)/F
0 for optimal detection, the better. As the initial concentration of SiO2@CDs@MIIPs increases, (F
0
–F)/F
0 gradually decreases. It reaches a critical point between 50 and 60 mg/L. The concentration of SiO2@CDs@MIIPs is also higher than that of SiO2@CDs@MIIPs. When the initial concentration of SiO2@CDs@MIIPs is higher than the threshold, the sensitivity of the detection system decreases. When the initial concentration of SiO2@CDs@MIIPs is lower than the critical point, the linear range of the detection system decreases. Therefore, the optimal working concentration of SiO2@CDs@MIIPs was determined to be 55 mg/L to take into account the overall performance of the fluorescence detection system.

To investigate the effect of different pH values on the fluorescence intensity of SiO2@CDs@MIIPs, 55 mg/L SiO2@CDs@MIIPs aqueous solution with pH values from 2.0 to 13.0 was configured as the solution to be tested. The fluorescence intensities of SiO2@CDs@MIIPs at different pH values were measured after the solution was left to stand until the fluorescence of the solution to be tested did not change significantly. As can be seen from Fig. 4d, the fluorescence quenching of SiO2@CDs@MIIPs is more evident under acidic conditions. The fluorescence intensity of SiO2@CDs@MIIPs is more vigorous when the solution pH is 7.0–9.0. Therefore, SiO2@CDs@MIIPs are better suited to work in solution environments with a pH of 7.0–9.0. Fig. 4e shows the fluorescence intensity change of a 55 mg/L aqueous solution of SiO2@CDs@MIIPs over 60 min of understanding conditions. From the figure, it can be learned that the fluorescence intensity of SiO2@CDs@MIIPs aqueous solution did not change significantly within 60 min. The fluorescence intensity of SiO2@CDs@MIIPs is stable for 60 min, which meets the detection requirement. In order to clarify the time required for SiO2@CDs@MIIPs to complete the detection process, the change in fluorescence intensity of SiO2@CDs@MIIPs with time under 50 μM Cu2+ was examined. As shown in Fig. 4f, the fluorescence of 55 mg/L SiO2@CDs@MIIPs quenched rapidly under 50 μM Cu2+ and remained relatively stable after 3.0 min, so the response time of SiO2@CDs@MIIPs to Cu2+ can be determined as 3.0 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on the detection performance of SiO2@CDs@MIIPs for Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para:
The detection performance of SiO2@CDs@MIIPs against Cu2+ is the primary objective of this study. The overall examination process must be carried out in the optimal working conditions identified in 3.2. A Cu2+ solution with a concentration gradient of 0–100 μM was used to measure the solution. SiO2@CDs@MIIPs and SiO2@CDs@MNIPs, as well as SiO2@CDs@IIPs as a control, were dispersed into different concentrations of solutions to be tested. The concentration of the three fluorescent polymers was determined to be 55 mg/L. Subsequently, the fluorescence intensity was measured at an excitation wavelength of 297 nm. The data were analyzed using linear regression analysis to plot the standard curve of Cu2+ concentration versus the fluorescence sensor. The linear regression equation was established by fitting the Stern-Volmer equation. Fig. 5 shows the fluorescence spectra of SiO2@CDs@MIIPs (a), SiO2@CDs@MNIPs (c), and SiO2@CDs@IIPs (e) at Cu2+ concentrations of 0–100 μM (insets show the actual fluorescence color change of the assayed solutions at the corresponding concentrations) and their corresponding linear plots. As shown in Fig. 5a, when the Cu2+ concentration increases from 0 to 100 μM, the fluorescence intensity of SiO2@CDs@MIIPs decreases. The fluorescence color of the detection solution produces a corresponding change. The linear relationship between the fluorescence quenching of SiO2@CDs@MIIPs and the Cu2+ concentration (c) was investigated using the fluorescence quenching efficiency F
0
/F-1 as the vertical coordinate. As shown in Fig. 5b, the linear equation for SiO2@CDs@MIIPs is F
0
/F-1 = 0.00903[c] + 0.01356 with a correlation coefficient (R
2 ) 0.9957. In order to determine the detection accuracy and applicability range of SiO2@CDs@MIIPs, the limit of detection (LOD) equation LOD = 3 δ/S and the limit of quantification (LOQ) equation LOQ = 10 δ/S were used to calculate the detection and quantification limits of the fluorescent sensor, where δ is the standard deviation of the blank background signal, n = 10. S is the slope of the linear curve, and the detection limit of SiO2@CDs@MIIPs was 5.74 μM. The quantification limit was 19.13 μM, so the detection range of SiO2@CDs@MIIPs can be determined as 20–100 μM. From the above results, it can be concluded that the fluorescence quenching behavior of SiO2@CDs@MIIPs is correlated with Cu2+. It has a good linear relationship with Cu2+ concentration, which can be used to detect Cu2+ in aqueous environments.

The fluorescence response and linear relationship of SiO2@CDs@MNIPs to Cu2+ concentration are shown in Fig. 5c and d. The weaker fluorescence decreasing trend can be attributed to the absence of imprinted recognition sites for Cu2+ in SiO2@CDs@MNIPs. Thus SiO2@CDs@MNIPs do not possess selective recognition of Cu2+. Quenching of CDs only by adsorption of Cu2+ through the mesoporous structure, the corresponding SiO2@CDs@MNIPs showed essentially unchanged fluorescence color in the presence of different concentrations of Cu2+.The linear equation for SiO2@CDs@MNIPs is F
0
/F-1 = 0.00281[c]+0.00983 with a correlation coefficient (R
2 ) 0.986. In addition, the fluorescence response behavior of non-mesoporous imprinted SiO2@CDs@IIPs to Cu2+ is shown in Fig. 5e and f, where SiO2@CDs@IIPs exhibit a more pronounced fluorescence quenching of fluorescence in response to Cu2+ concentration. However, different from SiO2@CDs@MIIPs, the fluorescence intensity of non-mesoporous imprinting decreases rapidly but remains unchanged when the concentration of Cu2+ is higher than a certain concentration, which can be attributed to the adsorption limit of thin layer imprinting layer.

Natural water bodies contain a variety of cations and anions. To examine the influence of the ionic environment of the water body on the detection effect, some of the common coexisting ions in the water environment were selected to include Na+, Ca2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Cr2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Al3+, Ni2+, SO4 2−, CO3 2−, HCO3 −, Cl− and NO3 − as the interfering ions, the effect of ionic environment on the detection effect of SiO2@CDs@MIIPs was investigated. As shown in Fig. 6 a, most of the common coexisting ion pairs have negligible impact on the fluorescence intensity of SiO2@CDs@MIIPs. However, the interference effect of Fe2+, Zn2+, and Pb2+ is more evident because the amino group of the functional monomer APTES, which mainly plays the role of coordination, can form coordination with the above metal ions. At the same time, APTES can recognize the above three kinds of ions. Although the fluorescence of SiO2@CDs@MIIPs is interfered with by a small number of ions, its quenching effect on Cu2+ is the highest, indicating that the presence of the imprinted layer can improve the selective recognition of Cu2+ by SiO2@CDs@MIIPs and improve the anti-interference ability of the sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on the mechanism of fluorescence quenching
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the fluorescence quenching phenomenon induced by Cu2+ during the detection process using SiO2@CDs@MIIPs, fluorescence lifetime analysis and UV absorption spectroscopy were employed to explore the underlying quenching mechanism between the imprinted polymer and Cu2+ ions. The fluorescence lifetimes of the carbon dots (CDs) before and after interaction with Cu2+ are shown in Fig. 6b. After the addition of Cu2+, the fluorescence lifetime of the CDs is significantly decreased, indicating that Cu2+ effectively quenches the fluorescence through non-radiative energy transfer or coordination interactions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of response time and detection limit
ce:section.ce:para: In order to compare the fluorescence detection performance of SiO2@CDs@MIIPs, some of the single-emission IIPs used for detecting Cu2+ were selected to compare with that of SiO2@CDs@MIIPs. The detection range, detection limit, and response time of the IIPs were taken as the main parameters. The results of the comparison are shown in Table 1 . The comparison in the table indicates that the mesoporous structure of SiO2@CDs@MIIPs shortens the response time to Cu2+, but there is still much room for research on the linear range and detection limit.

This will test the reliability of the established fluorescence detection method for SiO2@CDs@MIIPs and evaluate the possibility of using the technique for practical sample recommendations. Deionized water, river water and electroplating wastewater from factories were used as research subjects. The detection capability of SiO2@CDs@MIIPs in actual samples was verified by the spiked recovery method and direct detection and compared with ICP-MS results.

As shown in Table 2 , the recoveries of spiked Cu2+ from deionized water and tap water by SiO2@CDs@MIIPs were 97 %–112 % and 93 %–109 %. The RSDs were 3.4 %–4.2 % and 2.7 %–3.9 %, respectively. Compared with ICP-MS, the recoveries and RSDs of SiO2@CDs@MIIPs are still different, but the detection results generally agree. In the direct detection of electroplating wastewater, the concentration of SiO2@CDs@MIIPs was similar to that of ICP-MS. The comparison shows that SiO2@CDs@MIIPs can accurately and rapidly detect Cu2+ in actual samples.

In summary, a novel mesoporous ion-imprinted fluorescent sensor (SiO2@CDs@MIIPs) was developed for the efficient detection of Cu2+ in aqueous environments. By integrating mesoporous silica nanospheres with blue-emitting CDs and ion-imprinting technology, the sensor achieved a detection limit of 5.74 μM, a linear detection range of 20–100 μM, and an ultrafast response time of just 3 min at an optimized concentration of 55 mg/L. The mesoporous structure enhanced ion accessibility and fluorescence stability, leading to improved selectivity and anti-interference performance, as validated by experiments in tap water and river water.

The results confirm the sensor's reliability for practical applications, offering a robust platform for on-site Cu2+ detection in environmental monitoring. This work provides valuable insights into the design of functionalized mesoporous nanomaterials and highlights their potential for developing efficient and cost-effective fluorescent sensors. Future studies may focus on enhancing the sensor's selectivity for different metal ions, broadening the detection range to include both lower and higher concentrations of Cu2+, and improving its stability in complex environmental conditions. Additionally, strategies to further lower the detection limit and optimize the sensor's performance in real-world applications could significantly expand its applicability in diverse monitoring scenarios.

Jiaqi Li: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Lingxi Zhao: Investigation, Formal analysis. Tianyi Zhang: Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis. Zhu Yang: Methodology, Investigation. Xiangnan Liu: Investigation, Data curation. Bo Hu: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization. Lun Zhao: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Guangbo Che: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This article was supported by the Natural Science Foundation Project of Jilin Province (YDZJ202502CXJD010, 20250601066RC) and the Science and Technology Development Planning of Jilin Province, China (No. YDZJ202501ZYTS382).
